answer:
I now have a complete picture. Here's my answer:

# What happens when you call `response.content` on a streaming response

## The short answer

If you got the response via `client.stream(...)` (or `httpx.stream(...)`) and you haven't yet called `.read()`, then accessing `response.content` **raises `httpx.ResponseNotRead`**.

From `httpx/_models.py` (lines 635-639):

```python
@property
def content(self) -> bytes:
    if not hasattr(self, "_content"):
        raise ResponseNotRead()
    return self._content
```

The `content` property is a pure cache lookup — it never reads from the network itself. It just returns `self._content` if it has already been populated, otherwise it raises. The error message (`httpx/_exceptions.py:340-350`) is explicit:

> "Attempted to access streaming response content, without having called `read()`."

This is by design. The docs (`docs/quickstart.md:375`) state:

> If you're using streaming responses [via `iter_bytes`/`iter_text`/`iter_lines`/`iter_raw`] then the `response.content` and `response.text` attributes will not be available, and will raise errors if accessed.

## Lifecycle you need to respect

Looking at `iter_raw` (line 935) and `close` (line 961), there are three boolean flags governing the response stream:

- `is_stream_consumed` — set `True` once you start iterating the stream
- `is_closed` — set `True` when the stream is closed (auto when iteration finishes, or via `close()`)
- `_content` — exists only after `read()` (or full iteration via `read()`) has buffered the body

The rules enforced in `iter_raw`:

```python
if self.is_stream_consumed:
    raise StreamConsumed()
if self.is_closed:
    raise StreamClosed()
```

So your options when working with a streaming response are:

### Option 1: Iterate the stream once
```python
with httpx.stream("GET", url) as r:
    for chunk in r.iter_bytes():   # or iter_text / iter_lines / iter_raw
        ...
# After this, r.content raises ResponseNotRead — the bytes were never buffered.
```
You can only iterate **once** — a second iteration raises `StreamConsumed`. Once the `with` block exits (or iteration completes), the stream is closed and any further iteration raises `StreamClosed`.

### Option 2: Buffer first, then use `.content`/`.text`
```python
with httpx.stream("GET", url) as r:
    r.read()           # consumes the stream and stores bytes in self._content
    print(r.content)   # now works
    print(r.text)      # also works
```

`read()` (line 876) is what populates `_content`:
```python
def read(self) -> bytes:
    if not hasattr(self, "_content"):
        self._content = b"".join(self.iter_bytes())
    return self._content
```

### Option 3: Conditionally read based on headers
The pattern shown in the docs (quickstart.md:378):
```python
with httpx.stream("GET", url) as r:
    if int(r.headers['Content-Length']) < TOO_LONG:
        r.read()
        print(r.text)
    else:
        for chunk in r.iter_bytes():
            ...
```

## Async equivalent

Use `await r.aread()` instead of `r.read()`, and `aiter_bytes` / `aclose` / `async with client.stream(...)`. Same rules apply (`_models.py:974` onward).

## Summary of the lifecycle

1. `client.stream()` returns a response with headers but body unread; `_content` is unset.
2. You must either (a) iterate exactly once with `iter_*`, or (b) call `read()`/`aread()` to buffer.
3. `.content` and `.text` only work **after** `read()`/`aread()` has populated `_content`.
4. After consumption or closing, attempts to re-stream raise `StreamConsumed` / `StreamClosed`.
5. Always use the `with` / `async with` context manager so the connection is released even on errors.

378:>>> with httpx.stream("GET", "https://www.example.com") as r:

335	
   336	## Streaming Responses
   337	
   338	For large downloads you may want to use streaming responses that do not load the entire response body into memory at once.
   339	
   340	You can stream the binary content of the response...
   341	
   342	```pycon
   343	>>> with httpx.stream("GET", "https://www.example.com") as r:
   344	...     for data in r.iter_bytes():
   345	...         print(data)
   346	```
   347	
   348	Or the text of the response...
   349	
   350	```pycon
   351	>>> with httpx.stream("GET", "https://www.example.com") as r:
   352	...     for text in r.iter_text():
   353	...         print(text)
   354	```
   355	
   356	Or stream the text, on a line-by-line basis...
   357	
   358	```pycon
   359	>>> with httpx.stream("GET", "https://www.example.com") as r:
   360	...     for line in r.iter_lines():
   361	...         print(line)
   362	```
   363	
   364	HTTPX will use universal line endings, normalising all cases to `\n`.
   365	
   366	In some cases you might want to access the raw bytes on the response without applying any HTTP content decoding. In this case any content encoding that the web server has applied such as `gzip`, `deflate`, `brotli`, or `zstd` will
   367	not be automatically decoded.
   368	
   369	```pycon
   370	>>> with httpx.stream("GET", "https://www.example.com") as r:
   371	...     for chunk in r.iter_raw():
   372	...         print(chunk)
   373	```
   374	
   375	If you're using streaming responses in any of these ways then the `response.content` and `response.text` attributes will not be available, and will raise errors if accessed. However you can also use the response streaming functionality to conditionally load the response body:
   376	
   377	```pycon
   378	>>> with httpx.stream("GET", "https://www.example.com") as r:
   379	...     if int(r.headers['Content-Length']) < TOO_LONG:
   380	...         r.read()
   381	...         print(r.text)
   382	```
   383	
   384	## Cookies
   385	
   386	Any cookies that are set on the response can be easily accessed:
   387	
   388	```pycon
   389	>>> r = httpx.get('https://httpbin.org/cookies/set?chocolate=chip')
   390	>>> r.cookies['chocolate']
   391	'chip'
   392	```
   393	
   394	To include cookies in an outgoing request, use the `cookies` parameter:
